This morning what we're going to get started with is we're going to bring Greg back up. Greg, it's funny, every time I give Greg like 30 minutes, it turns into an hour and 15 and people don't want him to stop because he's got so much content. But he's going to kill you and slay you with content again today. Take notes because I mean of all the things that you need to do, Greg will show you how he's doing it for other people and he's going to give you some pretty cool X factors this morning. So, get excited and give a warm welcome for Mr. Greg Roulet. All right. All right, all right, all right. Thank you my friend. All right, so who was at the bowling alley and had him bowled in 10 years? 20 years. 30 years. It was fun seeing you guys last night and having a really, really good time and just meeting and mingling with each other. I think one of the biggest underutilized things about this whole event is the people that are sitting next to you, sitting behind you, sitting in front of you. I appreciate everyone coming up and chatting to me, telling me your story and it's incredible the talent that we have in the room today and the stories that you guys have to share and the aspirations and ambitions that you guys have and it all comes back to the one key fundamental that I'm talking about today which is how to actually become a celebrity expert which is something that we all really want to become in our businesses whether we know it or not. We want to be the guy that people turn to for advice. We want to be the guy that people turn to when they have a problem, when they want that problem solved and we want them to think about no one but us. That's what my presentation is about this morning. If you can pull it up there, Mr. Rami is the 8x factors of today's celebrity expert. We're going to be talking about some mindset things. We're going to talk about how to create your story today. We're going to talk about superpowers and superheroes and how you can become the superhero in your own business because it's really cool at events like this, we've positioned ourselves. Nick, Lindsay, Jack and myself as celebrity experts for what we do and how we position ourselves and it's incredible how people come up to us and they know my life story better than I know my own life story. Through newsletters, through emails, through videos that we shoot, before I even talked yesterday and told my own origin story about being the rapper in the rock band, people already were asking, hey, can I get your music on iTunes? Hey, how's your baby? Hey, you weren't here last year, your baby's turning one. They knew all of these things about me. They had these conversations. They knew about the products and services that we offered before they even stepped into the room and that's a really, really powerful thing because I don't have to explain myself. I don't have to explain what we do, why we do and how we do it and that is the power of the celebrity expert. So the first X factor of becoming a celebrity expert is believing that you are a celebrity expert and I don't have a clicker. So it's just imaginary right now. We'll just click. At least I knew what the first thing was. Thanks, boss, man. There he is. So the first X factor is believing that you are a celebrity expert and that's really, it sounds very simple on the surface level but when you are telling your story for the first time, when you're sharing your vulnerabilities with people for the first time, when you're telling people about your failures and how you've overcome them for the first time, it's really scary and we have limiting the beliefs as to what is actually possible. We get very complacent in our businesses. When I first started the product pros with Nick and Jack a few years ago, we got some great clients on board, we were doing some great business, creating some great products and I kind of got complacent. I was really happy with kind of where the lights are off now, it's party time. It's going to turn into a club in a little bit. So when we first started the product pros, I got complacent. We had a few clients, cash flow was okay, we were making a little bit of money but there was no desire for this growth. There was no burning desire behind the things that we were doing and then all of a sudden my wife says, hey, we're going to have a little guy in a couple months. And all of a sudden the fire just starts burning inside of you and now you really have to step your game up because you're no longer providing for just yourself, you're providing for a family and all of a sudden I had to really have a change in mindset to just being complacent, just going through the motions to we need to create something awesome, we need to create something that people can rally behind, something that people can get excited about. And that's what we've done over the past year or so with the Celebrity Expert Marketing Company is building something very powerful, something that people get excited about since the news of my son has come in, business has doubled or tripled in that time frame because now I have this burning desire. I believe in myself that I'm a celebrity expert and that the things that I can do can really change people's lives and that's what you need to think as well. And it's kind of difficult because you're just like, you know, I'm just a personal trainer, I got a couple clients, I'm not changing the world. You are changing the world because if you can help them get healthy, get back on track, they can get the new job, they can be more confident, they can find the man or woman of their dreams, they can get that pay raise, they can, you know, live longer, live healthier and you're just a personal trainer. You know, a lot of financial advisors in the room, you guys are changing people's lives, you're allowing them to live and do the things that they want to do in their life so believe in yourself and get over yourself. Get over your limiting beliefs, be able to share your story. I was talking to some folks over the last few days talking about their story and they're kind of scared to share their story. You need to get over that and get over yourself and we're going to talk a lot about creating your story here. Today, you need to get over those things, get over the limiting beliefs that you have about who you are and what you do. People really relate to you and the things that you have to say, the things, the places that you came from in the background and we're going to go through some really fun exercises for that today. So, skipping right into number two, you get paid for who you are and not what you do and that is a very, very difficult thing for people who are first coming into this concept to understand because they think that they're a dentist, they think that they're a lawyer, they think that they run a marketing business, they think that they're a web designer but it's not. It's you as a person as an individual. If I lined up six dentists right here behind me, I would assume that all six of them could clean my teeth and do a pretty good job at it. But there's one of the six that I'm going to relate to, one's going to have a child the same age as mine, one's going to do CrossFit like I do, one's going to run half marathons, one's going to have a huge family, one's going to have a small family and those are the small things that no longer make it transactional but make it a lifelong commitment of me working with this person. It's because of the stories that I have with them and that person, I don't care what the price point is at that point in that stage and that's something very critical for you to understand because celebrity experts don't fight for price. They fight for the people that they want to work with, the people that they attract into their business. Price no longer becomes an option when you are the only solution, when you are the one that they are knocking down the doors to work with. So typical dentist office has posters like this when you walk into the room and even worse is when they have like the, this is what your teeth look like and it's like the most disgusting photo in the world and you're so scared that they're going to break out this like chisel before you walk in the door. I want you to floss your teeth. There's no personal relationship with your dentist at that point. But what if you were Dr. Scott Schumann, one of our clients who is now the celebrity expert dentist. He gets paid for who he is, not what he does. Anyone can do what he does. But if you click that video right now, I want to show you kind of his office and his personality and the things that he does in order to position himself as, no one cares what this guy charges. They can't wait to be around him. They can't wait to see the crazy shenanigans he's going to do. He's at NASCAR races. He's at baseball games. He's at football games. He's at the Grammy Awards. And this is him giving an office tour. So, Rami, go ahead and hit play on that. Hey, you are in profitable dentist. Dr. Scott Schumann from grossedendental.com. We have the 8,000-5,000 winner. That's me, small office, only five operatories. But I'm part of the top group of fastest growing companies in America, 2010 and 2011. There's only a few people that can say that. Let me tell you how many people I've met. I'm going to be the first to know. I'm going to be the first to know. I'm going to be the first to know. I'm going to be the first to know. Let me tell you how many people I met. I'm a top group of fastest growing companies in America, 2010 and 2011. There's only a few people that can say that. Let me tell you how many people. I was 2,231 out of 5,000. I was number 184 in the health industry, number 26 in Columbus, Ohio. Hey, listen, he asked me a question. How can you use a book for dental marketing? Come over here, we have another book. Shift Happens. This is my first one. And listen, I got more than one. That's my first two. I got four. Uh-oh, no. I got five. More than a mouthful happened this week. Amazon best selling for down. So come on in here. Let me show you something else. American Premier experts, Inc. 500,000 again. And listen, guys, you can do this. Tell your story. Shift happens. Give a little advice how you can run your business and your practice better. Game changer. And here's my marketing secrets. But I don't want to tell you any of those. Listen, bottom line's this. Why would you do this? Because my business card is bigger than yours. See y'all. So this guy's got a personality. You guys are instantly attracted to him. He's from Grove City, Ohio. Yeah, they know how. But he has a really good time. He has fun. He's got a nice little NASCAR race, and he'll shoot a little video with his iPhone and be like, what's up, Dr. Scott Schumann, Grove City Dental here hanging out at NASCAR today and we're hanging out. I can't wait to fill and drill your teeth this weekend and going on and on and on. He shoots us out to his mailing list. That's the guy you want to be your dentist because you walk into his office, you're not walking into a dental office anymore. You're walking into a celebrity's office who happens to want to work on your teeth. Right? Another example. This was, I did a Google search when I was putting together this presentation for retirement planners in Atlanta. And on the fourth or fifth page, I came across Wilson Lewis, no offense to Wilson Lewis who is hopefully not here. And this is his retirement planning page and his whole entire website about retirement planning. That doesn't really excite me to send over a couple hundred thousand dollars to invest for my retirement. This doesn't exactly scream, whoo, can't wait to work with you. It's going to be a fun retirement. Look at this guy. But then you have someone like Phil and Jennifer Calandra, rock stars in their marketplace. They show off their celebrity, their personality. They're here, come and talk to them. Just ask how they create an environment that people are so excited to work for them. They get tons of people coming to their customer appreciation events. They take photos of them. They send the photos to everybody. They showcase everything. They've done the TV shows. They've had a documentary shot on their company. They have newsletters that go out and they talk about things in their newsletter like, hey, we just had our first grandchild. Hey, we just did this in the community. We're giving back. We're doing all these fun things. You know, here's a photo of some client events that we did. Here's our core story. These guys are people that you want to hang out with. You want to bring your friends to hang out with them. You want to bring your friends to their client events because they're cool people to be around. They're getting paid for who they are and not what they do. Very, very important distinction. This guy, he's kind of like a schlub on the red carpet. He's not all that good, right? But you guys are going to get this guy tonight on the red carpet because I get paid for who I am, not what I do, right? No Ryan Seacrest. I thought it was a fun joke. So who you are, not what you do is even more true and more powerful, the higher that you go up the affluence ladder, the higher caliber of client that you want, the more it's about a personal relationship because they want the guy, right? They want the person. Once you get high up the affluence ladder, they don't want to sift through 25 DVDs and 87 manuals. They just want to know, hey, I got Jennifer and Phil. Hey, I got Mary Lisa. She's taking care of my dental work. I don't have to worry about it because she is the best in the world at what she does. I don't have to worry about it anymore is what they really want to know. And that only comes from a personal relationship. That doesn't come from statistics. That doesn't come from features and benefits. That doesn't come from a long list of bullet points of what you serve. They only remember the stories, the stories about you, the stories that you create in your life and in your business. They want the guy. They want the guy to just take care of it. Take this problem away from me and make it happen. That's what you've hired us to do. You said, I'm going to hire Nick. He's going to make me a best seller. I don't have to worry about it because he guaranteed that I'm going to be a best seller. I don't have to worry about the process. We just took care of it. Do you do that in your own business? Do you take care of a huge problem in your marketplace? They want the guy to take care of it so they never have to worry about it again. All right. So, baby-proofing our house. A lot of different ways you can go about baby-proofing your house. So, we can go to Target, we can go to Amazon, we can just buy a bunch of random stuff and the little plugs and we can kind of do it ourselves. And the only thing I know how to do in my house is change a light bulb. So I'm not sure that I'm the guy that I want baby-proofing my own house. I can go to Babies Are Us or Buy Buy Baby. Now I'm getting to a specialty store that just serves babies in the baby community. So I'm going to pay a little bit more than I would pay on Amazon. And I can ask someone who has a little bit of specialized knowledge, but there's still kind of minimum wage employees there that are working there that are now going to help protect the safety of my child. I could buy a book or watch a DVD from an expert and I'm going to pay a little bit more for that, right? Hire up the affluence ladder, a little more help, a little more personalization. I can hire an expert as a consultant and say, hey, you know, I think that you can help me figure out all this stuff because I can't change a light bulb and I need to make sure that my baby doesn't electrocute himself. At the last stage, I can just hire this expert to do it for us. Switch out the baby stuff for your product or service. And this is how you create an ascension ladder in your business. You start at the generic, the free report, the CD, the seven tips, the whatever it is, is the Target or Amazon, right? Then you have some more specialization. So now it's just for a certain type of person. So seven steps just for golfers. Seven steps just for, you know, singers and dancers. Seven steps just for this type of person. So it's more specialization. Then you create products, programs and services based around that, books, DVDs, CD programs, coaching programs. Then you become a coach or a consultant, right? So I'll tell you what to do. The last stage is just do it for them. That's what people really want today is they want to know that you're the guy and that you're going to do all of this for them. So replace baby stuff with whatever you do in your business. And now you have an ascension ladder. You have a business model that works. And if you get to the bottom place on this ladder, price no longer becomes an object for you in your business. So X Factor number three. This is something that Jack talked about yesterday. It's something that I wholeheartedly believe in is that you are a marketer and a publisher, not a insert your business here. You think you're a dentist. You think you're an insurance agent. You think you're a real estate guy. You think you're a personal trainer. But if no one's in your door, you could be the greatest dentist in the world, but who cares? It doesn't matter. So I love this quote from David Ogilvy. He's one of my kind of heroes in the advertising world. He said, you can't save souls in an empty church. You can't. I can't share these messages to an empty room, but I got 200 bestselling authors that I can share some awesome stuff with. The hell of a lot better than sharing it to five or 10 or 20. But you know how we did it? We marketed our asses off to get you guys in this room. Do you know how many times Nick was away from his wife and his kids? No many times I was on the road. No many sacrifices we make to do the tele seminars and write the copy and do the follow up and send the direct mail and make sure it's in the newsletter and make sure that we're following up and make sure that our sales guys are calling and going out and getting leads. We marketed our butts off to do this event. And you really need to understand that you're a marketer of your services. You are not your services. And that's a mindset shift that's really tough when you've been in your profession for 10, 20, 30, 40 years. And you're wondering why people aren't coming in the door. It's because you're focusing on the thing. You're not focusing on the marketing of the thing. So myself, this is going to sound a little crazy, but this is what I do on a weekly and monthly basis just to promote myself. Right? I have the celebrity braiding you podcast that Nick and I record. It's a video podcast. We do it twice a month. I have the product procast. I do that once a week. It's a 45 to 60 minute show that I do once a week and put it on iTunes. We have the celebrity expert insider, which is our newsletter. I write two articles, both are over a thousand words in each article. So another two articles. I now have the CXM letter, which you guys have in your hands. That's either 12 or 16 pages of content that I wrote. I have the backstage pass, which is another 16 pages of content that I write. I don't outsource this. I write it. It's my voice. It's my stories. We do magazine ads. We're running four, five, six, seven magazine ads that we're writing from scratch along with the sequences that go with it every single month. I have emails to my celebrity expert marketing and product pros list. Right now, product pros are getting two emails a week. Celebrity expert marketing is getting one every week, sometimes two every month in that time frame. So those are more emails that I'm writing. Doing one to two webinars. And my webinars, if you guys have been on them, they're brand new. Every single time I do a webinar, it is brand new content. Because that's what people want. They want new. I don't want to see the same thing again. You guys don't want to see the same presentation I gave last year. You want to see something new. Every webinar I do, brand new content, brand new stories, brand new examples. One to two tele-seminars that I'm doing with Nick. Nick and I are doing a new tele-seminar on Tuesday. Brand new. We got a scripted it from scratch. One to two live events that I'm going out and I'm speaking at or I'm networking at and I'm joining things. The YEC, the Young Entrepreneur Council, they have promo articles. They do about 30 little snippets of articles to get on distribution places all over the internet every single month. Celebrity Expert Marketing Minute Videos. Every Friday my assistant will comes in and we shoot five Celebrity Expert Marketing Minute Videos. And then we distribute on YouTube. We do it every Friday morning. He brings me five little sample things. We get the camera on and I shoot five videos. And then we distribute them Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday of the following week. I do guest podcasts. I was interviewed last week three times for three other people's podcasts. And that's spreading my message now to their group. Right? And some people say, hey, that's a lot of time that you're investing. But these are people that I never would have touched if I didn't make the time to get on these different shows. Right? Guest blogs and guest articles. I'm writing one to two guest blogs on other people, other influencer sites every single month that drives traffic back to what we're doing. Sales letters and direct mail. I'm writing marketing copy that's going out every single month. Special reports and catalogs. The catalog that you have, the CXM Magazine that was on your chair when you guys came in. That was 12 pages of content that I wrote. And the past three weeks or so we put that together. All right? Personal letters. Personal letters and notes. If you guys aren't using those, you know, crazy. And then just random testing. I do random stuff all of the time. That was two slides worth of stuff that I'm personally doing. And here's just some of the results of that. On the left, that's a blog that I wrote for Fast Company Magazine, drives traffic back to the site. On the right, that's one of the snippets that I write for the Yonge Entrepreneur Council. And it gets syndicated on Inc. Magazine's website. In the middle, you can see one of the featured articles that we placed in, I think that was Fast Company or so. Podcasts on the blue one that's up there. That's Mixergy. Guided by the name of Andrew Warner. He has over 200,000 people listening to his show every single day. Do you think getting in front of that kind of audience would help your business? I'll make the time to do the interview. On the right, Entrepreneurs on Fire is now the third rated podcast in all of iTunes. You think that's going to help drive some traffic back to my business? Is it worth two, three hours of my time? You bet it is. And now I can use these as leverage points to get more of them. It is worth my time because I know I'm a marketer of my stuff. If I'm not doing this stuff, I'm not driving traffic back to what I am doing. I'm a marketer first. And that's just for me. That's not any of the client work that I'm doing. That's not any of the DNA stuff that I'm doing. That is just what I'm doing for myself and my brands. It's a lot of time invested, but time is well invested. More importantly, each piece has a purpose. You're not just doing this to do it. You're not doing it to go through the motions. You're not doing it to stroke your ego, even though most people I do think I do it to stroke my own ego because I like hearing my own voice and I like talking out loud and I like being on stage for a long time. But each piece of content has a very specific purpose. I'm trying to position myself in a certain way. I'm trying to have people hear me in a certain light. I'm trying to associate with certain people to leverage it to the next place. It's being in front of my market, my prospects, my clients, and my industry as a thought leader. As being someone, whenever I do this, I help them format the title. And I say, Greg Rowlett, expert marketer, celebrity marketer, celebrity driven marketer, product pro, product creation expert, every single one of these places. So now, Mixergy to 200,000 people, Andrew Warner says Greg Rowlett is the product pro. He's the product creation expert. He's worked with Brian Tracy and Tom Hopkins and Dan Kennedy and Michael Gerber and all these folks to 200,000 people who know exactly who they are. It's positioning for me. So think about that. How can you take advantage of these things and use them in your business? Every piece builds my legend and my story. I want other people telling my story for me. I can only tell you, Lindsay talked about it with testimonials, I can only tell you how cool I am for so long until you stop believing it. But when someone else tells you how cool I am, it makes much more of an impact. They're telling my legend, they're telling my story. So I go to an event, I go to like a GKIC event and I have someone coming up to me and they said, hey, I was listening to Mixergy the other week and I was a crazy good interview and Andrew said I had to talk to you. I've won that business already because they're telling my story and my legend. Hey, you're the rapper, rock band guy who does this marketing stuff and I saw some of your examples. Just give me that. That's a heck of an easier close for me than, all right, I got to go through the whole song and dance and the whole spiel and here's what I do and build trust. I'd rather have someone build the trust for me and that's what these pieces of content do. So key point, you're not a blank, you're a marketer of blank. All right, X Factor number four, you solve specific problems for specific people. So some of the conversations I've had with some of you over the last few days, the first thing I say to you is we can make an info product. I can create a sales letter for you, but who are we sending this thing to? Who are you selling this to? And you're just like entrepreneurs. All right, there's a lot of you. There's a lot of entrepreneurs, right? Women. Okay, you know, writing a sales letter to women is very, very difficult. We want to send information, our content to very specific people and we want to solve a specific problem for them. So we're going to play a little game. You guys want to play a game? Hell yeah, let's do it. So some of you guys have seen this before. If you've been to some of our TV shows, it's one of my favorite exercises to do. So if you have a piece of paper, I want you to make a line from the top to the bottom. You don't have to get a new sheet. You can use it with a half a sheet, whatever you got to do. Just a line from the top to the bottom going straight down. All right, in this exercise you might have heard of it, it's the Homer Simpson exercise. It is good times. All right. On the top left hand side of your page, I would love for you to write the name of your favorite TV character, your favorite movie character, your favorite reality star. It could be Kim Kardashian, it could be a Breaking Bad, it could be, I don't know, whatever you watch, Stewie from Family Guy. I don't know what you guys do in your spare time. But write down your favorite TV character. All right, you got that? I want you to write down four or five very specific things that you know about them. Are they married? What do they wear? Where do they work? Who do they hang out with? What's their personality like? Who's their boss? What do you know about this person? Why are they your favorite character? Do they have kids? Do they not have kids? Are they tall? Are they short? Or do they have some type of handicap? What is it about these people? So I'll give you an example. Homer Simpson. He's married to Marge. He's Bart, Maggie, and Lisa. He works at the nuclear power plant. His boss is Mr. Burns. He hangs out at Mo's. He drinks Duff Beer. He goes to the Quik-K-Mart. Just go on and on and on about this yellow dude. I know on and on and on about this guy, but knowing those things that I know about Homer Simpson is got three kids in that Lisa plays the saxophone. Could I sell him saxophone lessons on a Facebook ad? Knowing that Bart has ADD, can I sell him some type of training program or course on how to fix child behavior issues or tutoring or whatever the case is? Could I put some Duff Beer advertisements on his, send him to his home and sell him some things? I know a lot about this guy. I know his probably his income range. I know that he's a blue collar worker. I know the things that he does for extracurricular activities. I can write a very, very powerful sales letter and message to him and I can fix his problems. All right? So who wants to volunteer? Who's got a really cool TV character? All right, hold on. I'm going to get a microphone because I want to get this on the, I want to get this on film today. So let's go, let's go. Say your name and tell me your character if you can hold the mic. Thanks. Yeah, stand up. Come on. So my name is Drew Schultz and my character, I'm sorry, I can't remember his name. Last time I watched television was three and a half years ago. I've been so busy doing this stuff ever since, but I'm happy. So last show I watched was 24. So Kiefer Sutherland's main character and Jack Bauer. Thank you. Yeah, Jack Bauer. Okay. So I know I think he was divorced and I think he had maybe a girlfriend he met along the way or something. I think he had a daughter. He breaks the rules and he was in the military. So there's a very finite amount of stuff. We could market weapons to him. We could market, you know, hey, if you want to throw out any kind of manual on how to stay basically somebody who thinks outside the box would have to market to him something that's nonconformist. What else? I love it. That's good. Give him a round of applause, everybody. All right, let's go to Lynn. Yeah. Thanks, Bobby. So Lynn, who's your TV character, movie character of choice? Hi. I'm Lynn Leach and my favorite character is Sheldon Cooper. Yeah. Good show. Good show. My favorite show. He's a physicist. He loves comic books. He's a computer geek. His girlfriend is Amy. She wasn't his girlfriend before just a friend who was a girl, but now she's his girlfriend. His extended friends are Raj Kuthrapali and Howard Walowitz. Penny lives across the hall. He's afraid of birds. I love it. Give Lynn a big round of applause for sharing that. That is powerful stuff. So you know his friends. You know where he works. You know that he likes comic books. You know all of these things about this person. So I would love to hear all of your television characters, but the next part of this exercise is more important than hearing who your favorite TV characters are because knowing all those things about TV characters is great. They don't help you make any money. So I think you guys know where this is coming. On the right-hand side, I want you to write who your ideal customer is, who gives you money, and can you tell me as many things about that person as I just did about Homer Simpson, as Lynn just did about Sheldon Cooper? Because that's powerful. If you don't know who they are, what they look like, what they hang out with, they have kids and they don't have kids, where do they work, what kind of money do they have, you're in big trouble. But if you do know that, it's really easy to market to these guys. You can buy lists, you can buy ads, you can find trade journals and publications, you can find people who are already marketing to this person. You can find out what their needs are and actually create products that solve their needs instead of just creating a product because you have some idea about something. Create a product that people want to buy because it solves a problem. So who's a brave soul that can tell me everything about their client? Bob, you got that mic? Come on up. Coming up, third row. We'll start second one in. Hey Tim Bolecky, you looking for the ideal client? Yeah, let's do ideal client. I'm a criminal defense lawyer, so I want a military client, 21 to 25 years old, facing felony charges, looking at 25 years or more, who has a wife and children he may lose if he goes to jail with wealthy parents who are willing to fund the entirety of the retainer at one point in time who don't ask a lot of questions, who just want results. That's my ideal client. Yeah, we've been a lot of applause, everybody. Love it. But do you think we can market to that person? Oh yeah. We can find lists of kids in the military, get down to a fluent level, and we can send some direct mail pieces right to them when they get in trouble. And we can do some real good work to help them keep their family safe and our country safe. They're free though. All right, go ahead. Hi, I'm Cindy McKay and I'm a psychotherapist and a life coach. And my ideal client would be somebody who's in their middle age, who's financially successful at the fallout of relationships. In other words, they've spent so much time on building their success that they haven't built their significant others in their lives and maybe ruined some relationships. So teaching them how to rebuild and repair those relationships and finding what is the most important thing in their life, usually it comes back to people and finding that for them. All right, let's get more specific before we give her a big round of applause. You're perfect for who I want to do this exercise for. So middle age, what's middle age? Give me a number. It's not really a number, it's a mentality. It's where they have figured out that what they thought was what they really wanted, which was success, financially successful is not. So are they an entrepreneur, are they a professional, are they like a C-level executive, what made them financially successful? Yeah, those things, whatever business they put their time into to become successful. And once they've reached that platform, found out that they really weren't fulfilled. And so are they married? Could be. Could be. So you see her, I hope you guys see her, I'm going with this, is that she can have one sales letter just targeted towards entrepreneurs who are married and talking about how to save that marriage before they sell their business, before they go on to the next level. You can have another sales letter to entrepreneurs who are not married, who haven't made the time. You can have another sales letter and segmentation to those that have aspire to an executive level. You have another one that's to sales professionals. So you can segment your marketing and talk specifically to them because if you talk to me about saving marriage, whatever it is, I'm not really going to relate to that. I'm in a happy marriage right now. But if you talk to me about, hey, you're a successful entrepreneur, you're burning the candle at both ends. She's still sleeping a little hungover this morning so I can talk about it. So segmentation in this is key. If you have multiple target markets, you need to send different messages to the different channels. And we're going to talk a lot more about that in a few slides. But get very, very specific. Right? There's a big difference between someone who's 35 and someone who's 55. There's a heck of a lot more pressure on certain areas. Do they have kids or they want kids or do they not have kids? So really think about that in your marketing as you have to get specific as possible. So I want one more and I want to come from this side of the room. Bobby, your choice. Making you make the decision. Be a nice guy. And I can't see you over there. So sneak over a little because there's a light that's blinding me. All right. My deal client is... What's your name? Jereau. Jereau? Yes. Awesome. So my deal client is an entrepreneur between the ages of 30 and 50. Has a family of one or two kids. Has a business that's been in for about one to two years, well, two to three years. He's stuck. His passion is gone. His fire is gone. He's married, could be married, not married as well. Has between 10 to 30 employees. Looking for opportunity for growth. Values leadership. Is willing to invest. Has a business that's doing between 500,000 to a million, which means he can pay me. So the guy who's got the CPA firms, you know, you could just do marketing just to them, just to financial practices, just to professional practices. You can bundle them all together. You can do brick and mortar businesses. You can do, there's different segmentation within the entrepreneurial niche. There's this thing that's called a buyer is a buyer is a buyer is a buyer is a buyer. I'm one of them, right? You can sell me, you know, any kind of info product and I'm going to buy it. So you can find entrepreneurs who have bought one coaching program and another coaching program and another or they've subscribed to the Wall Street Journal in the last 90 days and then they've also subscribed to Fortune magazine. You know that they are buying financial advice. They want to be more successful. So you can then target and segment based on those kinds of things. So really good stuff. So give them a big round of applause. Give everybody a big round of applause. Thanks for playing along. We're going to have one more play along in just a little bit, but I appreciate it. Thanks Bobby. You rock my friend. So this is something that we've created for Stefan from Magic Future. Magic Future is one of the most incredible pieces of software, a tool that I've ever seen in my life. It's funny when Stefan and I started working together, I said, this is the tool that I wanted to create about five, six years ago. It's all about finding your magic number, the number that you need to attain to where work is no longer a necessity. And the great part about the tool is you don't just get a number. This application is all based around setting goals and habits to achieve the things that you want to achieve in life because it's all about having a plan for your life just as much as you have a plan for your death. But what we've done is we've segmented within his business different places to go after this. We solve specific problems for specific markets. We just said, hey, everybody in this room needs goals. And everybody's like, yeah, yeah, yeah, sure. I need goals. Yeah, sounds good. But if we hyper-target it to corporations, to why do your employees need to set goals because of employee engagement and because when you engage your employees, you have happier employees, they produce better results. So now we've created this piece. It's an eight-page sales letter and a nice brochure-looking piece that we send directly to CEOs and we say, hey, we can help your company make more money by engaging your employees, by helping them create goals, by helping them create a magic future. We've also segmented and created different versions of this same sales piece for fitness professionals, for fitness trainers to give this tool to their clients because one of the biggest problems with personal trainers is they get somebody on the program, they sign up, they're all jacked up and they're excited and then they drop out after 30 days, 60 days. But if they have a plan and they're setting goals and they have habits and they have these different pieces, well, now they can achieve their goals and lose the weight that they want, look better than they want in their life. So we are segmenting within his product to just CEOs, then to just fitness trainers. We're also going out to other businesses and we're segmenting, but we're solving specific problems in those markets. His product is much bigger. He has huge visions for where he wants to go, but we need to have a narrow focus in order to sell this thing and get it off the ground. Solving specific problems for specific markets and then segmenting off of it. So these are what some of the inside pages are, the magic of appreciation, self-fulfillment within companies, magic of goals, really, really cool program right there. So segmentation equals success. If you are not segmenting in your business, you are missing out on a huge opportunity. One market, one problem, one sequence just for them. If we've done books in the last year just for dentists, it helps us to no end to send that to our fitness professionals. It only helps us if we send it to dental professionals. Segmentation is key. So let's talk about how to craft your origin story, how to craft your magic powers because if you don't tell people, it goes back to yesterday when I talked about, you have to tell people you're a best-selling author, you have to tell people you're super powers and you do that through media, you do that through book chapters, through being on TV shows, through being interviewed, through your newsletters, through CDs, through DVDs, through radio shows, through all these different pieces. You need to tell your story, tell your story, tell your story, tell your story. I've told my story hundreds if not thousands of times now. You know how many times Nick has told that pirate joke? I'm sure he's sick of it but he tells it because it's an origin story. It creates affinity. It lets people know who he is and where his background came from and it creates that relationship. So, core origin story elements. These are the pieces that you need to put into your story. Where are you from? Really simple. You're from a small town, you're from a big town. There's pros and cons to both. It's just in how you write it. Where did you come from? You're from a different country or are you from a small town, you're from a big town and you left the big town. Your parents, your family, your siblings, we all have a crazy family story. Even if we're the Brady Bunch, we have a crazy family story. But were your parents divorced? Did you never meet your mom? Did you never meet your dad? Did you lose a parent when you were younger or older? Did you move out and live with your grandparents? Did you just have a great upbringing? Do you have 72 brothers and sisters? Are you the Octomoms kids? Are you, everybody has a story with their parents, their siblings? Right? Lessons from your youth. Right? Were you bullied in school? Were you the bully in school? Sports stories. Right? Everybody's got that high school hero story. Unfortunately, mine's in the opposite direction. I was, the third string quarterback was a sophomore and got brought up to play varsity. And we're in the Game of the Week in Fort Lauderdale and we're in like the stadium down here. It's Lockhart Stadium down in Fort Lauderdale. It's like the Game of the Week and it's televised on the local cable access channel. And I hadn't taken snaps in two, three weeks. I was playing corner back on defense and they threw me into quarterback on offense because our first two quarterbacks had gotten knocked out with concussions. So here I am and we run the option and I'm a pretty fast guy. That was sarcasm. And the coach goes, whatever you do, don't pitch the ball. Right? So I take the snap, bake the handoff. And what do I do? I see this huge dude coming and I pitch the ball. So we're on like the 30 yard line going in. So we got 30 yards to go to score, right? And so I pitch the ball and this guy comes from out of nowhere, intercepts the pitch and runs at 70 yards. We're touched down on the other end. Guys, name is Abdul Haji, place with the Denver Broncos right now. So it's a different kind of sports story or lesson from your youth. But those stories, they get people to laugh. They get people to chuckle. They get people to buy in again to who you are, right? There's a whole bunch of football player, high school football players in the crowd laughing at me right now, knowing that something similar happened to them or they had a player. Now they're creating memories of the touchdown that they scored or the big player, the big game. All those things create affinity. They create a connection with people. So lessons from your youth. First job or first business, right? I started working in a target stock room when I was 16 years old. There's a place for that, right? If you're trying to tell your story of how hard work and how you created your own and, you know, you did it on your own, you didn't rely on anyone else, all that stuff has a place. Your first business has a place. This is huge. Vulnerability. You have to be vulnerable in your story. You can't be perfect. Lance Armstrong tried to be perfect in what happened to him, right? Tiger Woods tried to be perfect. What happened to him? But having vulnerability is okay. Admitting things, saying that you had problems or challenges, failures, things that didn't go great. It creates believability. It also creates the fact that, man, if this guy did it, I can do it too and that's really what you want in your story, right? Vulnerability is huge. Connection to your market. The next slide is all about creating a connection to your market. I call it affinity. You got to have these things that connect you with your marketplace. You can't be so distant that you don't know who your people are and have any connection to them. And then where does your magic powers come from? Where is your Krypton? Where is your own Krypton? Right? For me, it was creating sales letters in the music industry, selling products to musicians. If I could sell, you know, $500 products to musicians using my personality and my videos, I could do it to anyone. All right. Moving along. I got like seven minutes. Affinity in your origin story. He's not here at this event because he's throwing a client appreciation event at the Cincinnati Reds Stadium. So we created invitations that were baseball cards of him in front of the stadium looking like a baseball player. He's sending this to retired folks. They have an affinity to both the Reds because they're in that town, but also what did they do when they were growing up? They put baseball cards in the spokes of their bikes. It draws back memories. They don't remember the facts, the benefits, the 37 bullet points. So they remember, you know, Phil's mom made great matzah ball soup. I read that in the newsletter. I need to talk to Phil. So, unfortunately, I'm going to skip this because I have two minutes and like 47 more slides to go. So, X Factor number six. You look like a celebrity expert when you walk through the door. You got to look the part. So, I want to talk about costumes. Costumes and uniforms. I'm in costume right now, right? I like wearing nice suits, but heck, I'd rather be in basketball shorts and a t-shirt right now. I'm in LA. I'm in Hollywood. I'd rather be by the pool and some board shorts and a tank top. I'm in my costume, right? Costumes, uniforms, right? Tony Stark is a really, really cool guy, right? Playboy, billionaire philanthropist, right? Really, really cool. But if that guy was saving the world, we wouldn't believe him, right? I don't know about this guy, you know, blowing up bad guys and saving the world, but this guy's badass. I don't know. I'm scared of him. I trust him to save the world, right? All the changes to the costume. All the changes to the way that he looked and the clothes that he put on and the way that he presents himself. Think about that in your business. Costumes also change with levels of client sophistication. I started out selling products to musicians, right? And so my costume at that point was t-shirt, usually a hoodie and a hat. If you Google some of my, or YouTube some of my really old videos from four or five years ago, I had someone, actually it was Taylor Swift's manager called Nick and I a few weeks ago. Great, I love your videos, but you look like a crackhead. If you're in the internet marketing world, this dude, Frank Kern, you're familiar with him, he was a surfer dude on the beach doing all kinds of things with surfboards and, you know, t-shirt and jeans and baggy and he had long hair. Now he's selling high priced consulting services to businesses. He doesn't look like this dude, he looks like this dude. He changed his market, he changed his costume. Very powerful. The other thing that we don't think about is what do they do after they're excited? Right? So they raise their hand, they want to do business with you. You spent all this time on your magazine ad, on your newspaper ad, on your book, on your TV show, on your documentary, but what happens when someone gets it and they want to look at you? Right? They give a big thumbs up to these people when they come through the door. What happens when the phone rings? Do you sound like a celebrity expert when your phone rings? Does your receptionist blow the sail for you because she is not on the level that you are being a celebrity expert? We fix that problem in our business. We have an amazing team. Amy and Brittany are two of them. They answer the phones and they are rock stars. They make Nick and I look like rock stars even when we can't be there to answer the phone. They know what to do, they know what to answer questions, they know who to send it to. On your thank you pages, this is probably the worst thing in the entire world. You just shot this cool video, you got this free special report and then you give an automated infusions off page and it just says, don't check your email for your thing. That doesn't look like a celebrity expert. Blow them out the freaking water on the thank you page. Thank you so much. Here's some other resources. Here's some things you could do. If you need help right now, do this. Here's the next step in the process. On your website, Lindsay talked about it all day yesterday. Your website does not represent you. Go talk to Lindsay right now because your website is a gateway to you and what you do. Your auto reply emails. Someone signs up to your mailing list and they get something automated from MailChimp that says thank you for subscribing. Doesn't sound like a celebrity expert. Welcome them into your family. Give them the link that they wanted. Have some pictures and things. Send them other resources. Show up like a celebrity expert. In your office, you saw Dr. Scott Schumann's office. He looks like a celebrity expert. If you're not displaying this stuff in your office, you're crazy and you're welcome kids. When you're welcoming, someone just hands you over a $20,000 account and you send them an email and says hey, thanks for signing up. Something's wrong. Show up like a celebrity expert every step of the way. All right, I got to go really, really fast because I'm two minutes over. X Factor number seven. You know the money is in the follow-up and the relationships. It's not all about the front end. It's about the back end. The back end of your business is the most important part of any expert business. I don't care what you're selling. It's all in the back end. Gerald Lucas, where's my man Gerald? What up Gerald? This is a six-step sequence that we created for Gerald sending out into the mail which of the six pieces gets the most response? The last. Yeah, Taylor II father's in the sixth piece. So the first and the last, right? So the first piece and the last piece. And they go out what, weekly for six weeks? Something like that? Yeah. So every week for six weeks. Six weeks worth of postcards. So if we stopped after one, he wouldn't get half his sales that come in at number six. If you're stopping at one email, if you thought we sent out one email to get you guys in this room, you're crazy. It doesn't happen. It's in the follow-up. So here's an inbound call sequence that we've created for one of our clients. Someone calls or someone goes online and they go to a thank you page and on the thank you page it has a video from the doctor. It says thank you so much for signing up. Here's what's going to happen. This exact person is going to call you. Here's the phone number it's calling from. So you can put it in your caller ID right now so you know the number when it calls so you don't miss it because you don't answer numbers from people that you don't know. How many more people do you think answer the phone? Then the thank you email comes out with that same stuff and in the thank you email we also send them a special gift. After that thank you email they get the first call. And the first call is from someone they expected the call to have come from. As soon as that first call happens on that first call they get the address of that person and they send them a gift. The gift is a free copy of the best-selling book along with a sales letter that tells them hey you just spoke with one of my reps. Here's what's happening. I copy the book. Here's what I need you to do next. They get four emails over the next four days. So after the first call we have a schedule call four days later. It's systematic. Over those four days we send four emails. Each email leads them closer and closer to that next call. So when that next call comes they are jazzed up. They are jacked up and they sign up because they've been getting really cool stuff over the four days. That second call is scheduled on the fourth day. If they're still not ready after that call another sequence begins. Five postcards are sent weekly. Those five postcards are story-driven testimonials from the people. Then there's ten emails scheduled out over the next five weeks. Two emails going out over those next five week period. Then they get a last chance letter in the mail. They get a last chance postcard. They get a series of last chance emails. We're hitting these people for thirty days after they sign up almost every single day. Half the business comes in on that last chance postcard. You're sending just one email. You are screwing yourself out of money. You're leaving money on the table. If you think that people are just going to sign up right here right now today and you don't follow up with them you are crazy. Then they get on the general marketing list at that point. You guys have this sequence in the backstage pass so I'm not going to go through it. So please read that on your flight home. I went over a little bit of it yesterday. I break down that for you in your backstage pass. Colander Newsletter. This is the last newsletter where they talk about their granddaughter. Granddaughter. Oh look at that. It's hooking me up. All right so I got seven minutes left. All right let's keep rocking and rolling. So talking about their granddaughter right on the first page. Which one? You guys will get all that stuff. So they talk about things in this. The newsletter is another touch point for them. The newsletter goes out to their existing clients and say hey we really love you and appreciate you. Here's some things going on in our life. Here's some things going on in the community. Here's some opportunities for you to get involved. Here's some of the events that we did last month that you didn't get involved in and look how much fun your friends are having. You should have some fun too. Here's our Tiffany dinner that we're having just for our referral people. You're not. You haven't referred us to anyone yet. Oh my goodness. Here's some photos of the last Tiffany dinner. You need to be at this one. It's just constant reminders of this stuff. Giving them different mediums, different modalities. If you're only sending email and you're not doing direct mail you're missing out. If you're only doing direct mail and not email you're missing out. If you're doing tele-seminars and not webinars you're missing out. Different modalities. It's work. It's a lot of work. But if you want to become the celebrity expert that you want to be the reason why you're here in this room you put in the work or you find the people that can do the work for you. Alright. Last one. X Factor number eight. Multiple streams of income and ascension. This is key. This is what Jack talked about yesterday. Moving from left to right. It's really taking a large amount of people and following them down into a small amount of people that you really help as you build them through the ascension ladder. A lot of you guys are trying to start intellectual property businesses, information businesses, internet marketing, internet marketing, speaking, coaching, product services, all that stuff. This is going to be very, very helpful for you. So ascension. DNA ascension. The way that you come into our business, maybe it was through a book. Maybe this is the first thing that you've done. Then you get hit up with a catalog. Maybe you do a TV show. Maybe you jump into a big print publication. Maybe, and that's kind of the front end of the funnel. This is what Jack talked about yesterday. Jack broke it down. And then after you do that, well then there's the next tier. Now you're here at the National Academy of Bestselling Authors. Maybe you're going to join the National Association of Experts, Writers, and Speakers and come and join the XPs with us. After that, then we have a deeper level of, so the most people come through that part of the business. Then we start ascending a smaller number of people into back end services, which is working with maybe Lindsay, working with myself, getting a website done, getting a product done, us doing our marketing for you. After that level, then we have events and masterminds and different sessions. And it's a smaller amount of people. There might only be 20 people in a mastermind, but 1,600 people in books. Those 20 people are getting a lot of our time, energy, effort, and attention. We're ascending them and we're turning one customer into a great customer by offering them more products and services, taking more things off of their plate. The expert ascension, so the information marketing business. You guys, you start with a free report. Then you sell some type of a front end product, $27, $50, $100, $500. Then you upsell them into a second product, maybe that's $1,000, maybe that's $2,000. Then maybe you have a monthly membership program, like the backstage pass that you need to sign up for, because it's awesome. It's a monthly continuity. And now you're getting money from them monthly instead of one time, which is very powerful. Then you have group coaching, where instead of working one-on-one with someone, I can get 20 people, 50 people, 100 people on the phone at the same time, answer everybody's questions, and now I'm leveraging my time. Maybe if they pay a little bit more, then we go to one-on-one coaching. Maybe then we have live events and retreats. Working with a client right now who is in the fitness world, and they want to have retreats four or five times a year. They only want to get 10 to 15 people at these events, but charge $10,000 a person to come to these events. 10 people at $10,000 is a $100,000 weekend. You do five of those a year, it's a half a million dollar business. That's without the front end stuff. See how that math works? I only need 40 people in that program, 50 people in that program. If I can get people to come back to multiples, then I even need less people. If you're selling something for $100, how many do you need to sell to make a million dollars? $10,000? I can't do the math in my head. A lot. But at a $10,000 price point, you need a whole lot less. So that's why you move people up through the ladder and through ascension, is because you need less people to get the same results financially. Have that ascension in your business. Get mortar ascension. Just different products, different services. You only have one product. That's all you can sell to them. When I was in the music industry first selling my first products, I had $147 products. If you bought that, I was done with you. I had nothing left. I had to create more products and services for those people. So have more products and services. Joint ventures. If you're a CPA, can you now start a financial practices? Can you start a different vertical in the process to take that one client and do multiple services under one roof? You do online, offline marketing, branding, all this stuff all under one roof. Same thing. So this is my favorite lead generation strategy that's working right now. You guys want to see it live? Yes. This thing is rocking and rolling and every single one of you, no matter your business, can put this into play. It's really, really cool. So this is the method to my madness and it's very fun. So do a webinar. Tell a seminar. This could be a live event. This could be whatever. Some type of event that you're driving people to. In my case, I love doing webinars. So this is the landing page. Get inside, look how we create winning marketing ideas, blah, blah, blah. They register for the webinar. I show up like a celebrity expert so I don't just say, hey, thanks for the webinar. Put it on your calendar. I say, boom, here's a video of the five rules of celebrity expert marketing which tees them up for what I'm about to teach them and lets them know who I am, my background, my origin story, all right there before they jump on the webinar. So thanks for signing up for the live webinar. There's a whole bunch of copy underneath as well. So this is just the one screenshot. Thanks for signing up for the webinar. Then I actually do the webinar. I perform the webinar and I sell the thing. The thing in this case for this one was a product, an information product called the celebrity expert marketing masterclass, creating your calendar essentially. And it was like a notebook of a bunch of our sequences and a CD of me talking about how we create those sequences. Sold it for $297 on the webinar, right? The bonus is what I really wanted to sell. I didn't really care about this product. It's a great product. I stand by it. I believe it in 100 percent. But getting this product in their hands was only the beginning. What I really wanted was everyone that purchased at a 30 minute consultation with me. So two things happen. One is the person that buys thinks that they get the guru, the expert, the authority on the phone for 30 minutes. When you buy an information product, that never happens. You buy it and you're like, hoes, you're barely lucky if you can get a refund or if a link's not working, they don't fix it. They're actually going to get me on the phone for 30 minutes to talk about them and their business. But to me, this is the landing page they go to when they sign up. So again, I don't just send them to a link to sign up. I send them a video. I shot it and I broke down all the stuff that they get. But I call them, like on the phone. It's crazy to call people on the phone. Who thought of it? And their eyes, they get to get the guru, the expert, the guy on the phone. On my end, it's a sales call. They already know that I'm the expert. They bought the product. They've gone through the product. They love the product. Now they have a couple questions. In the product, I told them so much stuff to do, they're never going to implement it. And I just say, you know what? We got this thing. I could just do it for you. And they're like, where do I sign? It's magic. It is magic. But I put in points one through seven before that phone call. They knew my story, knew where I came from, listened to me on a webinar, watched a ton of videos from me. I got a bunch of free stuff in the process. Got the product. We're excited about the product. And then I ascended them right then and right there. It is magical, that process.
